details.text: Derivative: أَعُوذُ comes from عَاذَ – يَعُوذُ means to cling to someone for protection or to hold on and not let go due to the fear of a danger that is unseen. مَعاذ (Ma’aadh) from the same root refers to a place where you find refuge. It is commonly translated to mean to “seek refuge” however its meaning differs from اِسْتَعَاذَ (Ista’aadha) which is in the form (اِسْتَفْعَلَ – Is’taf’ala) and implies “seeking” refuge and protection. عَاذَ implies giving oneself up to the protection of Allah and being actively engaged in the act rather than seeking to do that.

Derivative: ٱلْفَلَقِ literally means “the splitting” or “the tearing or cleaving open”. It is from the verb فَلَقَ which means to split or tear something from something else or to separate something apart. It can imply something that when ripped open, something comes out from it, like the sprout of a plant from a seed or water from the ground. Here it is referring to the daybreak as the rays of light in the morning tear through the darkness of the night to mark the separation of the day from the night. فَلَقَ is also associated with the meaning of creation as life can be seen to come about through the process of something being made to split open and then bringing something out from that splitting. A Hadith has also stated that ٱلْفَلَقِ refers to a door in hell that when opened makes the fire blaze and in another Hadith, it is stated to refer to a prison in hellfire for the arrogant and tyrannical.

Derivative: قُلْ (Qul – “say”) can have a different literary rhetorical value depending on the context it is used in. One of the benefits of the command to “say” in the context here is that it kills the ego and someone’s false sense that he is not in need of protection.

Derivative: شَرِّ (Sharr) is the opposite of خَيْر (Khair). شَرِّ (Sharr) refers to something that is universally conceived to be harmful and evil. Specifically, it relates to something that is harmful.
Derivative: غَاسِقٍ comes from the word غَسَق (Ghasaq) which refers to the time when the night comes and darkness penetrates.

Derivative: وَقَبَ means to get dark and with darkness it refers to such a level of darkness that things become invisible. It is used for when something enters something and can no longer be seen. This implies that darkness hides things.

Derivative: النَّفَّاثَاتِ is the feminine plural of نَفَّاثَة and refers to women who engage in the practise of blowing on knots for the purposes of sorcery. It is in the emphasised form which indicates that they engage in the act over and over again. It comes from نَفَثَ means to blow accompanied by the release of spit.

Derivative: حَاسِدٍ is from حَسَدَ – يَحْسُدُ – حَسَدًا which means to be envious or to have envy. Envy is not restricted to wanting what somebody else has or being jealous of that but includes the wish that the person doesn’t have it and is accompanied by hatred. It is a main motive or factor for people to engage in evil acts. حَسَدٌ (envy) is a harm that in unseen in itself although its consequences can be seen.

وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ

- “And from (وَمِن) the evil (شَرِّ) of the envier (حَاسِدٍ) when (إِذَا) he engages in envy (حَسَدَ)” – The language indicates that the evil is the result of the envious person engaging actively in the practise of envy.
